Includes faculty scholarship, peer reviewed journals, digitized theses and dissertations, and yearbooks.

title: Loyola University Chicago Presidents
creator: Young, Kathy; Monaghan, Tyler
description: An exhibit detailing the history of the twenty-four Presidents (23 Jesuits and 1 layperson) who have guided Loyola University Chicago since its founding in 1870 as St. Ignatius College.
subject: Loyola University Chicago; presidents; Jesuits;
source: Loyola University Chicago Archives and Special Collections

title: Loyola Timeline 1870-2015
creator: Young, Kathy; Barbuschak, Chris; Howdeshell, Ashley
description: A historical timeline of Loyola University of Chicago 1870-2015. This timeline is based upon “Loyola University of Chicago: The First 110 Years” by Robert C. Hartnett, S.J., and Richard M. Menges; “One Hundred Years of Knowledge in the Service of Man”; “Highlights of the Development of Loyola University of Chicago, 1870-1977” by Robert C. Hartnett, S.J.; “Chronology of Loyola University of Chicago”; and “Loyola at 70” and an unpublished manuscript history of Loyola University by Joseph Roubik, S.J.
subject: Loyola University Chicago; history
source: Loyola University Chicago University Archives and Special Collections

title: Then and Now: 150 Years at Loyola University Chicago
description: For the past 150 years, Loyola University Chicago has been a vital part of Chicago’s educational world. Combined with the story of its now-affiliated neighbor from 1930-1991, Mundelein College, Loyola’s history speaks to the broader history of Catholic higher education, the college experience, and Chicago. Displays some of that history through the themes of social justice and faith, student life, and Loyola’s multiple campuses.
subject: Loyola University Chicago; Mundelein College; history; social justice; faith; student life
source: Loyola University Chicago Archives and Special Collections; Women and Leadership Archives

title: Hidden Loyola
creator: Young, Kathy; Howdeshell, Ashley
description: Exhibit based on the famous yearly tour of hidden architectural treasures at the Lake Shore Campus given by Michael Grace, S.J., the images in this exhibit highlight the great architecture of Loyola and how the campus has been transformed over the years.
subject: Loyola University Chicago; Lake Shore Campus; architecture
source: Loyola University Chicago University Archives and Special Collections

title: Loyola University Chicago Photographs
description: Images documenting the people, places, and events at Loyola Chicago.

title: Loyola University Yearbooks
description: The Loyolan, Loyola’s yearbook, was first published in 1924 and remained in publication until 1986. Primarily includes information about all the colleges and schools of Loyola, however, for some years the College of Commerce (now the Quinlan School of Business), the School of Medicine, and the Chicago College of Dental Surgery have separate yearbooks.
subject: Loyola University Chicago; The Loyolan; yearbook; student publications
source: Loyola University Chicago University Archives and Special Collections

title: Loyola News and Loyola Phoenix
description: Loyola’s student newspaper was established in 1924. It ran under the name the Loyola News until 1969. The name was changed to the Loyola Phoenix in 1970.
subject: Loyola University Chicago; Loyola News; newspaper; student publications
source: Loyola University Chicago University Archives and Special Collections

title: Loyola Theses and Dissertations
description: Digitized theses and dissertations from masters and doctoral students at Loyola starting in 1929 and continuing through the mid-90s, plus current documents dating to 2019.
subject: Loyola University Chicago; theses; dissertations

title: Loyola Course Catalogs
description: Course catalogs and bulletins of St. Ignatius College and Loyola University Chicago from 1870 to 2002. This collection includes catalogs for the College of Arts & Sciences, School of Social Work, Stritch School of Medicine, Niehoff School of Nursing, Chicago College of Dental Surgery, School of Business Administration, School of Education, Institute of Pastoral Studies, the Graduate School, etc.
subject: Loyola University Chicago; St. Ignatius College; course catalog
source: Loyola University Chicago University Archives and Special Collections
rights: Contact Loyola University Chicago Archives & Special Collections for permission to copy or publish.

title: Mundelein Foundations, 1929-1931
description: The Sisters of Charity of the Blessed Virgin Mary (BVMs) founded Mundelein College in answer to George Cardinal Mundelein’s call for a Catholic college for women in Chicago. The college opened its doors to its first students in 1930 as the world’s first self-contained skyscraper college for women.
subject: Mundelein College; construction; architecture
source: Women and Leadership Archives

title: Activist Mundelein: Civic Engagement at a 20th Century Women’s College
creator: Cullen, Anne E.; Ippen, William
description: This exhibit traces the history of student activism at Mundelein College from the 1940s through the early 1970s. In particular, the exhibit shows the ways in which students took part in the Antiwar and Civil Rights Movements, which eventually came to a head in May 1970.
subject: Mundelein College; history; activism; antiwar; Civil Rights Movement
source: Women and Leadership Archives

title: Women and Social Justice
description: This collection focuses on the contributions of women through a variety of social justice activities in the 20th and 21st Centuries in the United States including women’s rights, civil rights, peace movements, workers’ rights, homelessness, poverty, business ethics, and healthcare reform.
subject: social justice; activism; women's rights; Civil Rights Movement

title: Feminism in Chicago: The Connie Kiosse Papers
description: This collection focuses on the Women’s Liberation Movement of the 1970s through highlighting documents from the Connie Kiosse Papers. Kiosse was an active member within Chicago’s Women’s Liberation movement throughout the 1970s and was among the founding members of The Feminist Voice, one of Chicago’s first feminist newspapers.
subject: Connie Kiosse; feminism; activism; Women's Liberation Movement
source: Women and Leadership Archives

description: One of the largest dairy farms in Lake County, Hawthorn-Mellody served the North Shore as far South as Evanston. The farm occupied more than 1,200 acres of land owned by the Cuneo family at the height of its popularity and production.

title: The Ellacuría Tapes: A Martyr at Loyola
creator: Berger, Dina; Macica, Katie; Salatka, Albert
description: In May 1986, Loyola University Chicago awarded Ignacio Ellacuría S.J., rector of the University of Central America in San Salvador and a well-known liberation theologian, an honorary doctorate. Three years later, he was one of the six Jesuits, along with their housekeeper and her daughter, brutally murdered in their shared home on the university’s campus.
subject: Loyola University Chicago; Ignacio Ellacuría; Jesuits; Salvadoran Civil War; Latin America; Salvadoran Martyrs
location: San Salvador, El Salvador; Chicago, Illinois
source: Loyola University Chicago Archives and Special Collections

creator: Jankowski, Eric; Young, Kathy

title: Raymond V. Schoder, S.J., Slide Collection
description: The Schoder digital collection is a selection from the slides taken by Rev. Raymond V. Schoder, S.J., who taught in the Classical Studies department at Loyola University. The Schoder collection consists of 17,000 slides taken from approximately 1950 to 1986 and includes classical sites of Ancient Greece, Ancient Rome, and Ancient Egypt; sites in the Middle East, Asia, South America, Europe, Soviet Union, and the United States; architecture; and art.
subject: Raymond V. Schoder; classics; travel; architecture
location: United Kingdom; Algeria; Morocco; Greece; Italy
source: Loyola University Chicago Archives and Special Collections
